content:
Intermittent fasting (IF) has gained prominence over the last decade as a dietary intervention that modulates the timing rather than the composition of food intake, and it has emerged as a particularly active area of investigation for metabolic syndrome [REF:2]. As one strategy of fasting, intermittent fasting has shown consistent performance in relation to weight loss and relevant clinical improvements, attracting public attention in recent years [REF:4]. The research question addressed in this review — what are the proven health benefits and risks of IF in adults with metabolic syndrome — sits at the intersection of clinical efficacy, long-term safety, cost-effectiveness, and population-level generalizability, and it requires careful disaggregation of protocol families, endpoints, and populations.

## Defining the Intermittent Fasting Family

The term "intermittent fasting" encompasses a family of distinct eating-pattern protocols that differ in cycle length, caloric permissiveness, and behavioral complexity. Time-restricted eating (TRE) confines daily caloric intake to an eating window of typically 4-12 hours, with 16:8 (16-hour fast, 8-hour eating window) and 18:6 being the most widely studied variants. Alternate-day fasting (ADF) alternates days of unrestricted eating with days at ~25% of usual caloric intake. The 5:2 protocol involves five days of normal eating and two days of severe caloric restriction, typically 500-800 kcal on fast days. The fasting-mimicking diet (FMD) is a periodic five-day regimen at approximately 25% of usual intake that preserves a fat- and fiber-rich macronutrient profile. Across these protocols, the common mechanism is the periodic interruption of the fed state rather than a continuous daily caloric deficit.

Among these regimens, IF has shown consistent weight-reduction effects: pooled analysis found a mean weight reduction of 3.59 kg (95% CI: -4.59 to -2.59, p < 0.0001) in metabolic syndrome populations [REF:3]. This effect size is clinically meaningful and consistent with the broader position that metabolic syndrome may benefit from intermittent fasting by improving weight, cholesterol levels, blood pressure, and glucose control [REF:2].

## Defining Metabolic Syndrome

Metabolic syndrome is a clustering of cardiometabolic risk factors — central adiposity, dyslipidemia (elevated triglycerides and reduced HDL-C), elevated blood pressure, and impaired fasting glucose or insulin resistance — that collectively multiplies the risk of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes. Several harmonized diagnostic criteria are in common use, most prominently the International Diabetes Federation / American Heart Association harmonized definition, which requires any three of the five component criteria. The specific population that clinicians most frequently target with IF interventions is adults with metabolic syndrome, often with concurrent overweight or obesity and in many cases with pre-existing glucose-lowering or lipid-lowering pharmacotherapy.

The global rise in metabolic syndrome prevalence, driven by both lifestyle and demographic factors, has motivated the search for accessible, low-cost dietary interventions with measurable metabolic benefit. Against that backdrop, intermittent fasting has gained popularity as a dietary intervention to improve metabolic health [REF:2], both because of its intuitive framing and because of its low marginal cost at the consumer level — unlike pharmacotherapy or structured lifestyle programs that require continuous clinician time and drug costs, IF is largely a behavioral modification that requires minimal external resources to implement.

## Framing the Scope of This Review

Given the breadth of IF-metabolic syndrome evidence, this review organizes the evidence base along five axes. First, it characterizes short-term efficacy via pooled effect sizes from systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials, focusing on the endpoints most clinically relevant to metabolic syndrome: fasting blood glucose, HbA1c, HOMA-IR, LDL-C, IL-6, body weight, BMI, and blood pressure. Second, it interrogates the heterogeneity of the RCT evidence base — the dominant moderators of effect size variation (protocol type, intervention duration, sex, baseline BMI), the publication-bias evidence, and the GRADE certainty assigned to each endpoint.

Third, the review confronts the long-term safety evidence, and in particular the much-discussed 2024 American Heart Association Epidemiology and Prevention | Lifestyle Scientific Sessions abstract that linked <8-hour eating windows to a 91% higher cardiovascular mortality risk in the NHANES 2003-2018 cohort. Fourth, it examines the real-world observational counterweight: the Ramadan intermittent fasting systematic-review literature, which covers hundreds of millions of Muslim adults and reports predominantly null or mildly beneficial cardiovascular effects. Fifth, it considers the regulatory, economic, and industry context — the FDA/EFSA/EMA/Health Canada posture on IF as a therapeutic intervention, the FTC substantiation standard for commercial IF apps, the Burg-Peet cost-utility analysis of the fasting-mimicking diet programme in Dutch type 2 diabetes patients, and the real-world retention and adherence data from the leading commercial IF applications.

## The Argument of This Review

The argument developed across the sections that follow is that the short-term RCT evidence for IF benefit in metabolic syndrome is robust and replicable — multiple independent meta-analyses converge on statistically significant improvements in multiple cardiometabolic endpoints, with several endpoints achieving GRADE-high certainty [REF:3]. However, the long-term evidence is characterized by a tension between supervised short-duration RCTs showing benefit, population-level observational data including both the AHA 2024 cardiovascular mortality signal and the Ramadan null, and critical methodological limitations that inform how confidently we can extrapolate supervised-RCT effect sizes to free-living consumers across geographies and populations. A balanced clinical position, supported by the weight of evidence reviewed here, endorses IF as a promising intervention for appropriate adults with metabolic syndrome, while acknowledging the substantial remaining uncertainty about long-term mortality risk, population generalizability beyond Western adult cohorts, adherence sustainability beyond six months, and the specific mechanism by which benefits arise.

## Structure of the Remaining Review

Subsequent sections progress from pooled efficacy evidence through methodological considerations, long-term safety signals, mechanistic pathways, real-world evidence from commercial IF applications, regulatory and economic context, documented harms and disordered-eating risk, and finally clinical translation and research priorities. Each section draws on the quantitative evidence from the peer-reviewed systematic reviews and meta-analyses that anchor the research base, along with complementary primary trial, observational, and industry sources. The review concludes with a knowledge-gaps inventory that identifies the research priorities most likely to resolve the current uncertainties — in particular, long-duration pragmatic RCTs, low- and middle-income-country trials, head-to-head comparisons with pharmacotherapy, and mechanistic studies that can distinguish timing-driven benefits from calorie-driven benefits when total daily caloric intake is held constant.

## Key Findings

- Intermittent fasting encompasses TRE (16:8, 18:6), ADF, 5:2, and fasting-mimicking diet protocols, all of which interrupt the fed state on a periodic schedule rather than imposing a continuous daily deficit.
- Metabolic syndrome may benefit from intermittent fasting by improving weight, cholesterol levels, blood pressure, and glucose control [REF:2].
- Pooled analyses of IF trials in metabolic syndrome populations show a mean weight reduction of 3.59 kg (95% CI: -4.59 to -2.59, p < 0.0001) [REF:3].
- IF has shown consistent performance in relation to weight loss and relevant clinical improvements, driving growing public and research interest over recent years [REF:4].
- The research question requires disaggregation across protocol families, endpoints (FBS, HbA1c, HOMA-IR, LDL-C, IL-6, BMI, BP), populations (Western RCTs vs LMIC, Ramadan vs supervised), and time horizons (short-term RCT vs long-term observational).
